paragraphs.text: Three realms ofhuman life. Thefirst realm: the life ofthe body, its organs and needs; normal and excessive carefor the body.

How many questions you have come up with regarding my two previous letters! This shows you to be a lively, industrious and receptive pupil, and promises success. I shall write all the more willingly. But I will not answer everything for you right now; I shall leave the answers for the other times. I will occupy myself with what you are most occupied. You write, “Your words about the aspects, powers and needs of human nature are bringing me into myself. I am turning inward. I see some of it, but the greater part of it is at times obscure, at other times completely invisible to me. I desire very much to know what the spiritual, mental and physical aspects are in man, what the needs are for each, and how they are satisfied. I would very much like to keep myself on the level of human worth, of true human worth, such as is appointed to us by the Creator.” Excellent! You are touching the very foundations of our life, the explanation of which will give us the foundations for future discussions. For how could it be otherwise for man to live, if not in the way for which he was made. Once we have established reasonable concepts about man’s make-up, we will have the most reliable indication as to how he is to live. I think many people do not live as they should, because they think that rules about the worthwhile life are outwardly imposed, and, not 45

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE springing from man’s very nature, are therefore not needed by them. If they were certain that there were such rules, they would not transgress them and fall away from them. And so listen, if you will.

The body consists of various organs, each of which carries out its function, which is necessary for the body’s life. There are three major systems: 1) the digestive^ which includes the lungs, heart, arteries and veins, lymphatic vessels and multiplicity of other vessels, ducts and glands, which serve as the various conduits for the blood and other bodily fluids; the function of all of these is the nourishment of the body; 2) the musculoskeletal system, the function of which is inner and outer movement, and 3) the nervous system, the center of which is the head, and the spinal cord and system of ganglia, which are located underneath the abdominal and chest cavity, while the radicles of the nerves pass through the entire body; the function of these is sensory. When the operation of these functions and their interrelationship is in order, the body is healthy and life is not endangered; but when this order is upset, the body becomes sick and life is endangered. Each function has its own particular need, which, making itself felt by the living creature, demands satisfaction. The needs of the digestive, or alimentary and corporeal part are: food, drink, air and sleep; the need of the musculoskeletalpartis to tense the muscles, which need everyone feels after sitting for too long a period of time; following directly upon this is the need for movement, which compels one to walk, stroll or perform some sort of work; the need of the nervous part is the pleasant stimulation ofthe nerves of the entire body, such as the regulation of warmth and cold and the like, and especially the pleasant stimulation of the five senses, by which the nervous system comes in contact with the outside world.
All this, as you see, has to do with the body; the soul wo uld seem to have nothing to do with it. But because the soul, by
